content.title: The Bits and Bytes of Computer Networking
content.summary: The Bits and Bytes of Computer Networking is a foundational networking course from Google. It explains how devices communicate, how data moves across networks, and why that understanding matters for troubleshooting, monitoring, and security work.
topics_covered: Introduction to networking: core devices, LAN/WAN/wireless concepts, and the OSI and TCP/IP models.
The network layer: IPv4/IPv6, subnetting, ARP, ICMP, and routing.
The transport and application layers: TCP vs. UDP, ports, sockets, and common protocols such as HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, SMTP, and FTP.
Networking services: DNS, DHCP, NAT, VPNs, and proxies.
Connecting to the internet: ISP architecture, gateways, wireless, fiber, and cellular connectivity.
Troubleshooting and future directions: ping, traceroute, ipconfig/ifconfig, netstat, SDN, and IPv6 adoption.
relevance: Provides the baseline needed to interpret logs, network behavior, and protocol-level attack paths.
Helps connect networking concepts to firewalling, IDS/IPS, SIEM work, and incident response.
Makes it easier to reason about misconfigurations, lateral movement, exfiltration, and other network-dependent security activity.
key_takeaways: The TCP/IP and OSI models provide a practical framework for both communication and defense.
Routing, ports, and protocols are central to how modern attacks and mitigations work.
Security depends on understanding what normal network traffic looks like before abnormal behavior can be identified.
